name: Hardhead (SS-365)
events: This page dealing with the wartime history of USS Hardhead was created in September 2025.
[No deck logs of this submarine are currently available to us so therefore some details, particularly regarding trials and exercises, might be missing on this page.]

10 May 1944 Having completed an initial training period on Lake Michigan, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) departed Manitowoc for New Orleans via Chicago. From Chicago, down the Illinois and Mississipi Rivers, to New Orleans the passage was made in a floating dry dock.
16 May 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) arrived at New Orleans.
22 May 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) departed New Orleans for the Panama Canal Zone.

27 May 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) arrived at Coco Solo in the Panama Canal Zone. She transited the Panama Canal later the same day and arrived at Balboa to commence a training period in Panama Bay.
28 May 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (T/Lt. M.V. Evans, USN).
29 May 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (T/Lt. M.V. Evans, USN).
30 May 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (T/Lt. M.V. Evans, USN).
31 May 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (T/Lt. M.V. Evans, USN).
1 Jun 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (T/Lt. M.V. Evans, USN).
2 Jun 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (T/Lt. M.V. Evans, USN).
3 Jun 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (T/Lt. M.V. Evans, USN).
4 Jun 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (T/Lt. M.V. Evans, USN). Later USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR) also joined.
5 Jun 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).
6 Jun 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR) and USS Clark (T/Cdr. A.R. Manning, USN).
7 Jun 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).
8 Jun 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).
9 Jun 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).
10 Jun 1944 USS S-11 (T/Cdr. F.M. Smith, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).

11 Jun 1944 USS S-11 (T/Cdr. F.M. Smith, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR), USS SC 662 (?) and USS SC-682 (Lt.(jg) G.E. Hoyt, USNR).

12 Jun 1944 USS S-11 (T/Cdr. F.M. Smith, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR), USS SC 662 (?) and USS SC-682 (Lt.(jg) G.E. Hoyt, USNR).

13 Jun 1944 USS S-11 (T/Cdr. F.M. Smith, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR), USS SC 662 (?) and USS SC-682 (Lt.(jg) G.E. Hoyt, USNR).
18 Jun 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) departed Balboa, Panama Canal Zone for Pearl Harbor.
5 Jul 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) arrived at Pearl Harbor from Balboa, Panama Canal Zone. (1)
10 Jul 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.

11 Jul 1944 During 11/12 July 1944, USS Bowfin (T/Cdr. J. Corbus, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Carlson (Lt.Cdr. J.A. Stapleton, USNR) and USS Lyman (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Wilson, USNR). These included night exercises.
13 Jul 1944 During 13/14 July 1944, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor. These included night exercises.
15 Jul 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.
16 Jul 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.
18 Jul 1944 During 18/19 July 1944, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor. These included night exercises.
20 Jul 1944 During 20/21 July 1944, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor. These included night exercises.
22 Jul 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.
23 Jul 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.
24 Jul 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.
27 Jul 1944 Around 1330 VW/27, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) departed from Pearl Harbor for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Philippines.
She was escorted out until 2015 VW/27, by USS PC-580 (Lt. T.K. LaLonde, USNR).
Before proceeding to her patrol area she is to top off with fuel at Midway. Also, if needed, some voyage repairs can be made there. (1)
31 Jul 1944 Around 1015 X/31, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) arrived at Midway. Here she was taken in hand for some (voyage) repairs by USS Proteus (T/Capt. R.W. Berry, USN).
After completing the repairs and topping off with fuel and water she departed Midway for her patrol area around 1530 Y/31. (1)
12 Aug 1944 At 1836 I/12, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) entered her patrol area. (1)
18 Aug 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) torpedoed and sank the Japanese light cruiser Natori (5170 tons) about 200 nautical miles east of Samar, Philippines in position 12°29'N, 128°49'E.
The Katori was escorting the Japanese fast transport T 3 from Cebu to Palau. The fast transport managed to escape. [both links are offsite links.]

0054 I/18 - In position 12°32'N, 128°15'E obtained a contact with the SJ radar bearing 252° at a range of 22500 yards. Went to battle stations radar, changed course to 110°, went to flank speed and commenced tracking the target.
0115 I/18 - At a range of 16000 yards picked up a second pip on the radar screen, 1000 yards on the starboard quarter of the first and bigger target.
The target meanwhile had tracked on a base course of 085° at 17 knots, zingging 30° (70° and 100°) every 5 minutes.

0219 I/18 - With the target bearing 241° and a range of 12000 yards, slowed down and turned towards the track of the target for an attack with the bow tubes. The targets was sighted with binoculars at 8000 yards but details were impossible to make out except for a large tower mast and a large stack. The target appeared either a battleship or heavy cruiser at this time. The torpedoes were set to 10 feet in case it was a heavy cruiser.
0237 I/18 - In position 12°29'N, 128°49'E, fired five bow torpedoes from 3200 yards for a torpedo run of 2800 yards. No.6 torpedo tube was not fired as the outer door could not be fully opened.
On completion of firing the torpedoes, USS Hardhead gave full left rudder and all ahead flank for a stern tube shot.
0240 I/18 - The target turned on a large dim light on the foremast for about five seconds.
Shortly afterwards four stern torpedoes were fired from 2200 yards for a torpedo run of 1900 yards.

0242 I/18 - Explosions were heard. The second explosion was followed by a loud prolonged rumbling noise, punctuated by three more explosions, and two large simultaneous fires of about two to five seconds duration just aft of the foremast. Flames shot up more the two thirds the height of the foremast which showed it to be a battleship with a list of about fifteen degrees to port. The target slowed down and eventually came to a stop. The escort [or what was thought to be the escort but in fact was the fast transport, see above] which was never seen visually, continued milling around on the targets far quarter. It was considered possible that this ship may have been hit by one of the torpedoes that had missed the target as one of the lookouts later reported an explosive flash on the far side of the target.
0248 I/18 - Heard three explosions from the direction of the target. Radar showed that the 'escort' had closed the target.
0258 I/18 - Range to the target was now 8000 yards and the 'escort' was now heading eastwards in our direction at about 10 knots. The target was laying stopped.
0305 I/18 - The 'escort' was tracking on course 000° at 13 knots.

0328 I/18 - Completed reloading the torpedo tubes and followed the 'escort' for a possible shot at him. The escort was still on course 000° at 13 knots. When the range to the target was 14000 yards she disappeared from the radar screen.

0349 I/18 - Decided to go back to have another look at the 'battleship'. About this time decided that there was something screwy about the whole set up - a 'battleship' with only a single escort lets us get into a range of 3500 yards for the bow tubes and 2200 yards for the stern tubes without being detected, and doesn't shoot at us after he's hit. Then then the 'battleship' was stopped, the escort wanders off on a course at right angles to, the original course, apparently not paying any attention to the submarine. No depth charges were dropped. What kind of ship was this 'escort' ?

0400 I/18 - Picked up the target on radar at 12500 yards and commenced tracking. Slowed down to approach the target. The target was tracking on course 280° at a speed of around half a knot. After the range had been closed to 7000 yards decided it was definitely the battleship again. As the range closed, the target seemed to look different, probably due to his list. Decided to use depth settings of 6 feet to attack from the starboard side where his belt armour would be probably pretty well out of the water.
0441 I/18 - In position 12°29'N, 128°49'E, Fired six bow torpedoes from 3500 yards. Headed east to reload immediately after firing.

0444 I/18 - Head the first of a series of seven explosions accompanied by six explosive flashes along the targets waterline. The target soon faded out from the radar screen at a range of 13000 yards. Headhead changed course to proceed to the south and then west.

0538 I/18 - Changed course to 300° to close the range for another look at the target. The target appeared on the radar screen at about 13000 yards still in the same position. Daylight was approaching and it was tried to transmit a contact report so that aircraft could finish him off if he got away from Hardhead.

0554 I/18 - The target was sighted by lookouts and high periscope watch. His speed was zero. The target was definitely identified as a battleship with his bow almost awash and a big starboard list. The target had a tall Pagoda-type foremast, single tall stack and a fairly heavy mainmast well aft.
0559 I/18 - The target appeared to open fire as tracers and flashes were seen so dived and commenced a submerged approach.
0713 I/18 - When the range was slightly less than 6000 yards two explosions accompanied by rumbling were heard. The target had disappeared when the periscope was raised after about six minutes.
0717 I/18 - Several loud explosions were heard accompanied by rumbling noise.
0719 I/18 - Four distinct explosions were heard.
0721 I/18 - Three more explosions were heard followed by another explosion a minute later.
0729 I/18 - A final explosion was heard.

2158 I/18 - After cruising in the area for the remainder of the day during which large quantities of oil and wreckage were sighted, but no survivors, headed in the direction of the San Bernadino Straits at 15 knots. (2)

28 Aug 1944 At 0537 I/28, east of Leyte Gulf, in position 10°41'N, 126°05'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) sighted a ship bearing 050° from the bridge. It was too dark to observe details except the contact appeared to have a large angle on the bow. Slowed down and turned towards. When the range was 10000 yards the target was picked up on the SJ radar.

0539 I/28 - Dived and continued the approach on the target. The bearing was determined to be drawing left so went to normal approach course. The target was a fleet destroyer but it was too dark to make out details through the periscope. Targets course was 270° at 20 knots. The target was making 210 revs and was zigging radically. It was not possible to close to attack. He was subsequently seen to pass to the southward of Suluan Island and north of Homonhon Island.

At 1404 I/28 - in position 10°45'N, 126°05'E, sighted a puff of smoke. Further puffs of smoke indicated that this vessel was heading towards Hinatuan Pass between Bucas Grande Island and Mindanao. The contact was never sighted due to the long range.
At 1602 I/28 - USS Hardhead headed north to proceed to patrol off the Surigao Stait. (1)
13 Sep 1944 During 13/14 September 1944, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) conducted lifeguard duties off Leyte Gulf during air strikes on the region. Her services were not required. (1)
15 Sep 1944 At 1300 I/15, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) left her patrol area in position 07°30'N, 126°56'E, to proceed to Fremantle via the Molukka Passage and the Banda Sea. (1)

22 Sep 1944 At 1144 I/22, in position 18°02'S, 115°55'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) sighted what appeared to be a submarine through the high periscope on bearing 143° at a distance of about 15 miles. USS Hardhead turned away.
At 1158 I/22, the submarine disappeared over the horizon. Changed course to the eastward and commenced trailing.
At 1258 I/22, sight was regained on the other submarine. The periscope shears appeared to the USN-type.

At 1327 I/22, the other submarine slowed down so did the same. Attempted to exchange recognition signals with the SJ radar but was not successful. The other submarine appeared to be on a course of 060°.
At 1355 I/22, the other submarine suddenly disappeared most likely it had dived. Hardhead resumed her original course.
The submarine encountered was most likely the northbound USS Hoe (T/Cdr. V.B. McCrea, USN). (1)
26 Sep 1944 Around 1140 I/26, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F. McMaster, USN) ended her 1st war patrol at Fremantle, Australia.

She was escorted in by HMAS Tamworth (T/Lt. D.B. Lloyd-Jones, RANR(S)) which had joined around 0600 hours. USS Growler (T/Cdr. T.B. Oakley, Jr., USN) and HMS Sturdy (Lt. W.St.G. Anderson, DSC, RNR) also joined to be escorted in.

12 Oct 1944 Having completed her post patrol refit, during which the submarine was also docked, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) conducted a training period off Fremantle from 12 to 19 October 1944. (3)

15 Oct 1944 During 15/16 October 1944, USS Growler (T/Cdr. T.B. Oakley, Jr., USN), USS Hake (T/Lt.Cdr. F.E. Hayler, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) conducted 'convoy attack' exercises off Fremantle with HMAS Inverell (Lt. A.I. Chapman, RANR(S)), HMAS Parkes (Lt.Cdr. N.O. Vidgen, RANR), HMAS Warrnambool (Lt.Cdr.(Emgy.) E.J.B. Wight, RAN) and USS Isabel (Lt. E.G. Zacher, USNR). (4)

17 Oct 1944 USS Gunnel (T/Lt.Cdr. G.E. O'Neil, Jr., USN), USS Hake (T/Lt.Cdr. F.E. Hayler, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) conducted exercises off Fremantle with HMAS Dubbo (T/Lt. F.W. Roberts, RANR(S)), HMAS Inverell (Lt. A.I. Chapman, RANR(S)) and HMAS Parkes (Lt.Cdr. N.O. Vidgen, RANR). (5)
19 Oct 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) conducted attack exercises off Fremantle during which HMAS Parkes (Lt.Cdr. N.O. Vidgen, RANR) acted as target. (6)

21 Oct 1944 USS Gunnel (T/Lt.Cdr. G.E. O'Neil, Jr., USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) conducted exercises off Fremantle with HMAS Inverell (Lt. A.I. Chapman, RANR(S)). On completion of these exercises the submarines departed for their 7th and 2nd war patrols respectively. They were to patrol in the South China / Sulu Seas and off the Philippines respectively.
USS Hardhead was to form part of a coordinated attack group made up of, besides herself, USS Growler (T/Cdr. T.B. Oakley, Jr., USN) and USS Hake (T/Lt.Cdr. F.E. Hayler, USN). (7)

22 Oct 1944 At 1802 H/22, in position 27°55'S, 112°02'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sighted what is thought to be a southbound British submarine. It was attempted to exchange recognition signals but was not successful.
This must have been HMS Sturdy (Lt. W.St.G. Anderson, DSC, RNR). (3)

23 Oct 1944 Around 1800 H/23, USS Gunnel (T/Lt.Cdr. G.E. O'Neil, Jr., USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) parted company. USS Hardhead proceeded to Exmouth Gulf while USS Gunnel continued on to proceed to Darwin. (8)
24 Oct 1944 Around 0645 H/23, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) arrived at Exmouth Gulf from Fremantle.
After topping off with fuel she departed for her patrol area around 1320 H/23. (3)
27 Oct 1944 In the early hours, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) made the northbound transit of Lombok Strait on the surface. No Japanese patrols were encountered.

At 1615 H/27, in position 05°53'S, 116°44'E, a vessel was sighted, thought to be a destroyer or escort destroyer, bearing 358° at a range of about 18000 yards. Identification was doubtful. Went to flank speed and changed course to 240° to gain a position ahead. The course of the target was estimated as being 210° at 19-20 knots.
At 1702 H/27, contact was lost on the target which still could not be definitely identified. (3)

30 Oct 1944 At 2201 H/30, in the Sulu Sea, in position 08°18'N, 121°28'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) picked up T/Cdr. F.E. Bakutis, USN, from the sea. He was the Commanding Officer of VF-20 from Enterprise (T/Capt. C.D. Glover, Jr., USN). His aircraft, a F6F Hellcat, had been shot down by enemy AA fire while attacking a Japanese task force made up of the battleships Yamashiro, Fuso, heavy cruiser Mogami and the destroyers Michishio, Yamagumo, Asagumo and Shigure (all links to Japanese warships are offsite links). (9)

31 Oct 1944 At 0234 H/31, in the Sulu Sea, in position 08°44'N, 121°42'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) encountered USS Batfish (T/Cdr. J.K. Fyfe, USN) which was on lifeguard duties and searching for downed aviators.
As USS Hardhead was waiting to make rendezvous with USS Angler (T/Cdr. F.G. Hess, USN) she assisted USS Batfish in her search. (10)
1 Nov 1944 At 0013 H/1, in position 09°54'N, 121°33'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) exchanged recognition signals with the SJ radar with USS Raton (T/Lt.Cdr. M.W. Shea, USN). (3)

1 Nov 1944 At 0345 H/1, in position 10°23'N, 121°37'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) made rendezvous with USS Angler (T/Cdr. F.G. Hess, USN) to which T/Cdr. F.E. Bakutis, USN was transferred.
As USS Hardhead had only just arrive in the area and was at the beginning of her patrol and USS Angler was to return to Fremantle T/Cdr. Bukatus was transferred to her. (3)

4 Nov 1944 Around 2045 H/4, in position 13.20'N, 119.25'E, the coordinated attack group made up of USS Growler (T/Cdr. T.B. Oakley, Jr., USN), USS Hake (T/Lt.Cdr. F.E. Hayler, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) made rendezvous. Arrangements were made regarding the patrol of the assigned area. (3)

5 Nov 1944 At 1245 H/5, off the north-west tip of Mindoro, in position 13°21'N, 120°12'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sighted the tops of a PC-type patrol vessel bearing 069° at a range of about 6 miles. The enemy vessel was on a northerly course. (3)

8 Nov 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) torpedoed and sank the Japanese tanker Manei Maru (5266 GRT, built 1944) about 90 nautical miles south-west of Manila, Philippines in position 13°30'N, 119°25'E.
The laden tanker was en-route from Miri to Manila escorted by the destroyer Shigure and the frigates Chiburi and Kaibokan 19 (all links are offsite links).
0142 H/8 - In position 13°14'N, 119°42'E, sighted and obtained radar contact on a convoy of three ships, one large and two small bearing 095° at 16000 yards.

0143 H/8 - Commenced tracking and went ahead on three engines to gain position ahead. Determined the course of the target as 315° at 11 knots. Possibly there was a third escort well out on the starboard quarter of the target.
0151 H/8 - Received a message from USS Growler (T/Cdr. T.B. Oakley, Jr., USN) that she had radar contact bearing 097° at a range of 25000 yards. Her position was 13°28'N, 119°22'E.

0212 H/8 - All ahead flank, USS Growler was closing astern but the SJ radar was having difficulty keeping on the target at 15000 yards range. The leading escort was about 1000 yards ahead of the target. Another escort was 1500 yards on the port quarter of the target.
0219 H/8 - USS Growler reported enemy course 350° at 14 knots. She reported that she was tracking the enemy from the port bow of the convoy.
0232 H/8 - USS Growler reported enemy course 315°. Hardhead was ordered to attack from the starboard bow of the convoy.
0251 H/8 - With the target bearing 138° at 13350 yards, USS Hardhead dived to radar depth.
0253 H/8 - Heard and explosion. Possibly a torpedo explosion.
0254 H/8 - The target zigged right to course 030°. The escorts were difficult to follow either by radar or periscope.
Between 0257 H/8 and 0259 H/8 - Three depth charge explosions were heard.
0300 H/8 - Surfaced to regain position on the starboard bow of the target.
0321 H/8 - The target zigged left to 305°.
0328 H/8 - Submerged to radar depth. Range was 12000 yards. Targets course was now 270° but she made radical zigs and it looked like she was under constant helm.
0339 H/8 - Periscope depth. Range was now 8900 yards.
0357 H/8 - Range was now 1200 yards. The leading escort was only 500 yards away.
0359 H/8 - In position 13°53'N, 119°26'E, commenced firing four stern torpedoes from 800 yards.
0401 H/8 - Saw two and heard three hits on the target.

Shortly afterwards a fourth hit was heard and the leading escort was illuminated by flames. Went deep at full speed and rigged for depth charging. The target was identified as a laden tanker of around 9000 tons.
0402 H/8 - Heard a heavy explosion aft.
0403 H/8 - Heard breaking up noises astern. Fast screws and pinging was picked up all around.
0411 H/8 - Eleven depth charges exploded astern. Some light bulbs broke. Sound reported two, possibly three, escorts pinging. Commenced to clear the area to the south-west at 4 knots.
0620 H/8 - Started to come up. Pinging was soon heard and screws were heard astern. Ten or more depth charges were dropped all well astern. Went back down.
0800 H/8 - Two depth charges were dropped astern.
Between 0857 H/8 and 0901 H/8, Twenty depth charges were dropped far astern.
0917 H/8 - At periscope depth. Nothing in sight. Rigged for normal running.
0946 H/8 - Five distant depth charges were heard to explode.
1010 H/8 - A distant depth charge was heard to explode.
1035 H/8 - Many distant depth charges were heard to explode.
1047 H/8 - Five distant depth charges were heard to explode.
Between 1222 H/8 and 1225 H/8 - Twenty-nine distant depth charges were heard to explode.
1455 H/8 - Surfaced. Set course for the scouting position of tonight according to the orders received earlier.
1725 H/8 - After having submerged for an aircraft at 1644 H/8, seven distant depth charges were heard to explode.
1727 H/8 - Surfaced.

1952 H/8 - In position 14°11'N, 118°59'E, obtained radar contact bearing 124° at 20000 yards. Two pips appeared on the radar screen. Commenced tracking and went to flank speed to get ahead. The targets were on a westerly course at 18 to 20 knots.
2000 H/8 - The contact developed into two ships in column with a third about 4000 yards on the port quarter of the column. All gave large pips.
2030 H/8 - The targets slowed down to 11 knots. Their course was 260°.
2048 H/8 - Range was now 5500 yards. The targets were identified as PC-type escorts. Broke off the approach and headed for the scouting position.

9 Nov 1944 USS Hake (T/Lt.Cdr. F.E. Hayler, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) tried to communicate with USS Growler (T/Cdr. T.B. Oakley, Jr., USN) but there came no reply to their signals.
T/Cdr. Greenup then gave patrol orders to T/Lt.Cdr. Hayler until USS Growler returned.
USS Growler was however not heard of after yesterdays attack on the Japanese convoy and most likely the heavy depth charging heard may have accounted for her loss. (3)

10 Nov 1944 At 2025 H/10, west of Luzon, in position 15°28'N, 119°29'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) obtained radar contact bearing 018° at a range of 24000 yards. The target turned out to be a properly lighted hospital ship on a southerly course. (3)

12 Nov 1944 At 0420 H/12, west of Luzon, in position 15°30'N, 119°37'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) obtained radar contact bearing 115° at 11000 yards. The pips on the radar screen were very small. Tracked the contact on course 350° at a speed of 5 to 6 knots. Sound picked up two ships pinging. Nothing was in sight when the range had come down to 8000 yards. Avoided the enemy which were obviously patrol vessels.
At 0837 H/12, in position 15°27'N, 119°41'E, sighted the masts of a sea truck bearing 152° on a northerly course.
At 1031 H/12, in position 15°29'N, 119°42'E, a small landing craft was sighted on bearing 131°. The enemy vessel was on a northerly course. (3)
13 Nov 1944 At 0600 H/13, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) received orders from ComSubsPac for lifeguard duties during air strikes for later today and tomorrow.
At 0721 H/13, west of Manila Bay, in position 14°36'N, 119°03'E, smoke and a mast were sighted bearing 130° at a range of about 12 miles. USS Hardhead commenced tracking the enemy.

At 0728 H/13, USS Hardhead had to submerge for an approching aircraft. The contact was seen to be a convoy of three medium transports and three escorts in very loose formation. Enemy base course appeared to be 300° when Hardhead submerged which put her very close to the convoy's track. Commenced approach and went to battle stations.

At 0900 H/13, the convoy now tracked on a base course of 240° at 8 knots. The last ship in the column was bearing 150°. Range was now around 16000 yards. It was not possible to close to attack at standard speed so the approach was broken off.
At 1007 H/13, USS Hawkbill surfaced and proceeded to her assigned lifeguard station on 4 engine speed.
At 1018 H/13, a float plane was sighted 7 miles away so USS Hawkbill dived. Surfacing again at 1107 H/13. A contact report was sent on the convoy contacted earlier that morning.
At 1300 H/13, USS Hawkbill arrived in her assigned lifeguard station.
At 1306 H/13, in position 14°58'N, 119°36'E, masts were sighted bearing 151°. USS Hawkbill submerged and manned battle stations.
At 1345 H/13, the contact was identified as a hospital ship on course 350° at a speed of 14 knots. A practice approach was made on the vessel and also photographs were taken through the periscope.
This was most likely the Tenno Maru (6076 GRT, built 1927, former Dutch Op Ten Noort) which had just departed Manila for Takao (now called Kaohsiung, Taiwan). (11)

16 Nov 1944 At 0200 H/16, USS Hake (T/Lt.Cdr. F.E. Hayler, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) made rendezvous. Arrangements were made regarding patrol positions for the coming period. (3)

18 Nov 1944 At 1139 H/18, west of Luzon, in position 16°06'N, 119°40'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sighted two small enemy patrol vessels bearing 152°, range was about 7000 yards. Enemy course was 350° at speeds between 6-8 knots.

At 1202 H/18, while attempting to evade the enemy at periscope depth to the westward one patrol boat appeared to have gained contact. Went deep and rigged for silent running and depth charging. No depth charges followed though and eventually the patrol boats passed clear.
At 1350 H/18, USS Hardhead returned to periscope depth to find nothing in sight.
At 2052 H/18, USS Hawkbill proceeded to her lifeguard station for the following two days off Sampoloc Point to the north-west of Subic Bay.

At 2300 H/18, in position 15°37'N, 119°10'E, echo ranging was picked up on bearing 115°. The bearing drew left rapidly. No contact was obtained with the SJ radar. Sound reported two sets of echo ranging. Avoided these vessels which were obviously a Japanese A/S patrol. (3)

19 Nov 1944 At 0814 H/19, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) received a report that an aircraft had crashed in the water bearing 300°, 35 miles from Sampoloc Point. All ahead flank to proceed to the reported location.

At 1010 H/19, Ens. T. McCue, USNR of VF 20 from Enterprise (T/Capt. C.D. Glover, Jr., USN) was picked up. His fighter aircraft, a F6F Hellcat, had been hit in the engine while attacking Clark Airfield.
USS Hardhead subsequently returned to her lifeguard station but her services were not required during the remainder of the day. (3)

21 Nov 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) attacked what is thought to be a Japanese patrol vessel that was escorting a convoy of, most likely, landing vessel, west of Luzon in position 15°31'N, 119°36'E. Six torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained.

0236 H/21 - West of Luzon, in position 15°31'N, 119°36'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) obtained SJ radar contact bearing 099° at a range of 9000 yards. Sound heard two sets of echo ranging. Nothing was in sight but visibility was poor against the coast. Took evasive action.

0310 H/21 - Obtained SJ radar contact on bearing 109° at 20000 yards. Believed SJ tunning had been faulty on the first contact so commenced tracking and went ahead flank to gain a position ahead. Course of the target was 170° at a speed of 10 knots.
0325 H/21 - The target group was difficult to analyze but at 16000 yards appeared to be one large ship with several escorts. Sent a contact report to USS Hake (T/Lt.Cdr. F.E. Hayler, USN).

0350 H/21 - The target group was doubtfully identified as two escort vessels on the flanks of several small pips which were probably landing craft. Decided to attack the starboard screen from a position which would place other targets on the torpedo tracks.
0400 H/21 - 8000 yards the starboard screen was sighted and it appeared to be a medium sized escort vessel.

0417 H/21 - The target had zigged right 25° to course 195° presenting about a 75° starboard angle on the bow. With only 30 minutes to go before morning twilight headed in for an attack at 15 knots. When the range had come down to 4000 yards the target zigged back to 170° and the set-up looked poor. None of the other targets were in sight because of the land background. Decided to continue the attack though.

0427 H/21 - In position 15°31'N, 119°36'E, fired six bow torpedoes at a medium sized patrol vessel with a torpedo run of 2500 yards. The target was seen to alter course to the right about the time the torpedoes reached him and subsequently turned on a red masthead light and chased at about 14 knots. He was evaded withough difficulty.
0438 H/21 - Heard five or six end of run explosions. (3)

23 Nov 1944 At 0453 H/23, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) received a contact report from USS Hake (T/Lt.Cdr. F.E. Hayler, USN); ' Three targets bearing 145°, range 20000 yards from position. Enemy course was 240° at 15 knots.
By 1100 H/23, the convoy had not been contacted and had probably headed south for the Palawan Passage.

At 2008 H/23, in position 15°49'N, 119°34'E, SJ radar contact was obtained on a large and a small ship bearing 101° at a range of 17800 yards. Targets course was 010° at 12 knots. Cdr. Greenup decided to try to intercept off Rena Point and commenced an end around at flank speed.
At 2050 H/23, Off Caiman Point the target group was very close to the coast. It was difficult to maintain radar contact.

At 2110 H/23, radar contact was obtained on a second escort about 6000 yards ahead of the target. The escorts were seen to be signalling and subsequently took up positions 1000 to 1500 yards on the port beam of the targat and 2000 to 2500 yards ahead of the target.

At 2136 H/23, with the target now bearing 145° at a range of 13000 yards submerged to radar depth about 7000 yards off Rena Point and headed in to close the coast. Visibility towards the coast was very poor.

At 2158 H/23, doubtful radar range and bearing on target 118° at 6300 yards. Nothing was seen through the periscope a minute later. This placed the target very near the coast on course 020°. Sound conditions towards the beach were also very poor. Bearing on pinging from the beam escort showed the submarine to be about 1000 to 1500 yards from the estimated track.

At 2202 H/23, the port beam escort was sighted bearing 169° at a range of about 1000 yards. He seemed to have sound contact. The leading escort was bearing 060°. He stopped searching and commenced proceeding towards while pinging. The target could not be seen nor heard. He was most likely very close inshore.
At 2207 H/23, USS Hardhead went deep and took evasive action. The escorts stayed in the vicinity until around 2230 H/23 but no depth charges were dropped. (3)
24 Nov 1944 At 0100 H/24, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) received orders regarding lifeguard duty on 25 and 26 November.
At 1240 H/24, in position 16°20'N, 119°31'E, the mast of a patrol vessel was seen bearing 115° at a range of about 9 miles.
At 1302 H/24, the mast of another patrol vessel was seen bearing 100° and also a float plane on the same bearing. Range to the aircraft was about 8 miles.

At 1348 H/24, pinging was heard in the direction of Cape Bolinao. Also three aircraft were seen circling in that direction. They were gradually drawing south. USS Hawkbill headed west to clear the coast, surface and to commence an end around. No ships had been sighted so far.
At 1607 H/24, when about to surface an aircraft was seen at a range of 5 miles so, reluctantly, had to remain dived.
At 1810 H/24, USS Hardhead surfaced and set course to proceed to her lifeguard station for tomorrow off Sampoloc Point. (3)
25 Nov 1944 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) torpedoed and sank the Japanese frigate Kaibokan 38 about 60 nautical miles west of Manila, Philippines in position 14°33'N, 119°51'E.

Kaibokan 38 was escorting the naval stores ship / ammunition ship Mamiya (15820 GRT, built 1924) from Saigon to Manila. Other escorts were Kaibokan 14, Kaibokan 16 and Kaibokan 46 (all links are offsite links).

0822 H/25 - While west of Manila Bay in position 14°25'N, 119°54'E, the fighter cover reported a ship proceeding towards which was identified as a hospital ship. It was a large single stack ship which avoided on the surface.
1607 H/25 - The fighter cover departed, the lifeguard duties were over.
1835 H/25 - Surfaced and headed for our night patrol station off Manila Bay.
1908 H/25 - Submerged for an aircraft contact.
1924 H/25 - Sound contact on bearing 215°. This was thought to be an escort. Went to battle stations and surfaced.
1935 H/25 - In position 14°22'N, 119°57'E, obtained SJ radar contact on bearing 270° at 17000 yards. One large target was detected with other indefinite indications of more targets.
1939 H/25 - The target was tracked on course 100° to 115° at a speed of 12 knots which was a course to enter Manila Bay.

2008 H/25 - The target was now bearing 269°, range 12350 yards. Submerged to radar depth. Escorts were still indefinite, radar reported several small pips which seemed to jump around with relation to the target.
2010 H/25 - Went to periscope depth. An aircraft contact closed to 5 miles.
2015 H/25 - The target and two leading escorts were in sight. Escorts appeared to be about 2000 yards, broad on the bow of the target. Angle on the bow of the target was zero, range 9000 yards.
2025 H/25 - Sighted another escort nearly dead ahead close on the starboard bow of the target, angle on the bow zero. At this time the leading starboard escort was on Hardheads port quarter.
2027 H/25 - Changed course to the left to parallel the escort as soon as he commenced to draw right with a small angle on the bow.
2030 H/25 - Commenced to swing to the right to get onto a firing course.
2032 H/25 - Range was now 1250 yards. The target was a large transport of about 8000 tons.
2033 H/25 - In position 14°22'N, 119°57'E, fired six bow torpedoes from 1200 yards. Due to an error in the set-up all most likely missed ahead.
2034 H/25 - Fired four stern torpedoes at an escort which evidently been close on the starboard quarter of the target. Range was 1250 yards at firing.
2036 H/25 - Observed and felt a torpedo hit on the escort. The torpedo hit amidships and the escort appeared to disintegrate.
2037 H/25 - Sound reported a high noise level aft and brief breaking up noises followed by an explosion.

2040 H/25 - Looked the situation over and discovered that the target had changed course radically and was now on a parallel course on bearing 335°, broad on the starboard bow, angle on the bow 80P, range about 1500 yards. Ordered the bow tubes to be reloaded. The two leading starboard escorts were in sight well astern milling about.
2042 H/25 - Fired no.1 tube at the transport from 2000 to 3000 yards. The remaining three torpedoes were not yet reloaded.

2045 H/25 - Heard a torpedo explosion. Took a quick look at the target and saw what appeared to be a column of smoke on his starboard quarter. The visibility in the direction of the target was fair but a moderate sea was running. Time indicated a torpedo run of about 5200 yards.

2048 H/25 - Heard an explosion, source unknown. The original port escort, who evidently made sound contact when the target reversed course the second time, was coming in fast on the starboard beam. Changed course towards him at full speed, went deep and rigged for depth charging.
2051 H/25 - Six close depth charges were dropped astern.
2053 H/25 to 2057 H/25 - Eighteen depth charges were dropped, some of which were close.
2104 H/25 - Eight more depth charges were dropped, well astern.
0010 H/26 - Surfaced and set course for lifeguard station off Sampoloc Point. (11)

26 Nov 1944 At 0345 H/26, in position 14°31'N, 119°43'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) obtained SJ radar contact bearing 145° at a range of 11000 yards. Pinging from one source was heard on the same bearing. This was near the location of last evenings attack. Observed the radar screen closely but only could make out one small pip.
Later that night received a signal that the air strikes scheduled for today were cancelled.

At 0535 H/26, in position 14°31'N, 119°26'E obtained SJ radar contact on bearing 212° at a range of 12000 yards. Two small pips were seen on the radar screen and two small patrol vessels were subsequently seen.
At 0537 H/26, USS Hardhead submerged.
At 0902 H/26, in position 14°26'N, 119°21'E, a small patrol vessel was sighted bearing 090° which was avoided.
At 1029 H/26, USS Hardhead surfaced and set course to proceed to Mindoro Strait to leave patrol and return to Fremantle. (3)

28 Nov 1944 At 0118 H/28, in position 06°05'N, 119°44'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) and USS Flasher (T/Cdr. G.W. Grider, USN) made rendezvous. A follow-up motor for the TDC (Torpedo Data Computer) was transferred by USS Hardhead which was returning from patrol to USS Flasher which was en-route to her patrol area and had a defective TDC. (3)

28 Nov 1944 At 1426 H/28, in position 02°43'N, 119°40'E, USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN, with T/R.Adm. E.H. Bryant, USN on board) sighted a submarine through high periscope which was closed and turned out to be USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) which was on her way to Fremantle. Information was exchanged. (12)
29 Nov 1944 At 1804 H/29, in position 03°21'S, 118°26'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sighted a British submarine bearing 269° on a northerly course.
This was HMS Sea Rover (Lt. J.P. Angell, RN) which had also sighted the American submarine. (13)

30 Nov 1944 At 0622 H/30, in position 05°41'S, 116°57'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sighted a British submarine bearing 218°. Recognition signals were exchanged with HMS Sturdy (Lt. W.St.G. Anderson, DSC, RNR). (14)
30 Nov 1944 At 1852 H/30, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) commenced the southbound transit of Lombok Strait on the surface.

At 1900 H/30, in position 08°11'S, 115°52'E, a patrol boat was sighted bearing 239° at a range of 13000 yards. The enemy turned on a red masthead light and appeared to be closing. Avoided to the eastward at flank speed passing within three miles of the 100 fathom curve. The enemy was not picked up on radar.
At 2045 H/30, calls were exchanged with the northbound USS Hoe (T/Lt.Cdr. M.P. Refo, 3rd, USN). She was warned of the presence of the Japanese patrol vessel.
At 2135 H/30, USS Hardhead completed the southbound transit of Lombok Strait. (3)

1 Dec 1944 At 0958 H/1, in position 11°34'S, 115°17'E, the southbound USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) encountered the northbound USS Paddle (T/Lt.Cdr. J.P. Fitzpatrick, USN). She was warned about the Japanese patrol vessel encountered the previous day in Lombok Strait.

At 1104 H/1, in position 11°45'S, 115°15'E, USS Hardhead encountered the northbound Hammerhead (T/Cdr. J.C. Martin, USN). She too was warned about the Japanese patrol vessel encountered the previous day in Lombok Strait. (3)
5 Dec 1944 At 0830 H/5, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Fremantle.
She was escorted in (identity of the escort not known). The escort had joined around 0500 H/5.
Before entering harbour noise and D/G trails were carried out.
At Fremantle she was taken in hand for refit by a relief crew from Submarine Division 261 and by USS Anthedon (T/Cdr. R.E. Hawes, USN).
19 Dec 1944 During the period of 19 to 22 December 1944, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) conducted exercises off Fremantle. (15)

21 Dec 1944 During 21/22 December 1944, USS Besugo (T/Cdr. T.L. Wogan, USN), USS Blackfin (T/Lt.Cdr. C.L. Raguet, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) conducted convoy attack exercises off Fremantle. These included night exercises.

The simulated convoy was made up of the frigate USS Corpus Christie (Cdr. W.W. Childress, USCG) and the submarine rescue vessel USS Coucal (T/Lt. W.P. Baron, USN). They were escorted by the minesweepers HMAS Dubbo (T/Lt. F.W. Roberts, RANR(S)), HMAS Inverell (Lt. A.I. Chapman, RANR(S)), HMAS Mildura (Lt.Cdr. J.M. Little, RANR(S)), HMAS Warrnambool (Lt.Cdr.(Emgy.) E.J.B. Wight, RAN), armed yacht USS Isabel (Lt. E.G. Zacher, USNR), motor minesweepers USS YMS 11 (Lt. D.C. Maughan, USNR), USS YMS 72 (Lt. J.E. Reilly, USNR) and motor launches HMAS ML 815 (Lt. A.C. McAllister, RANVR) and HMAS ML 826 (Lt. S.A. Hancock, RANR).
Before commencement of the night exercises both motor minesweepers and both motor launches parted company. (16)

24 Dec 1944 Around 1600 H/24, USS Besugo (T/Cdr. T.L. Wogan, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) departed from Fremantle, both for their 3rd war patrols. They were ordered to patrol in the South China Sea and the Gulf of Siam where they were to form a coordinated attack group of which USS Blackfin (T/Lt.Cdr. C.L. Raguet, USN) was also to be part of.
T/Cdr. Wogan was the leader of the group.
Before proceeding on patrol night exercises were carried out with HMAS Warrnambool (Lt.Cdr.(Emgy.) E.J.B. Wight, RAN), USS Isabel (Lt. E.G. Zacher, USNR) and USS Coucal (Lt. W.P. Baron, USN).
The exercises commenced around 1900 H/24 and were completed around 0300 H/24 when both submarines set course to proceed to Exmouth Gulf. (17)

27 Dec 1944 Around 0650 H/27, USS Besugo (T/Cdr. T.L. Wogan, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) arrived at Exmouth Gulf where they both topped up with fuel before proceeding towards her patrol area later the same day.

Both left Exmouth Gulf around 1930 H/27 to proceed towards their patrol area via Lombok Strait. Before departing the Gulf both carried out gunnery exercises on the training hulk moored inside the Gulf.

28 Dec 1944 At 0129 H/28, in position 20°21'S, 113°56'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) exchanged recognition signals with the SJ radar with the southbound USS Bashaw (T/Cdr. R.E. Nichols, USN). (3)
29 Dec 1944 At 0604 H/29, in position 14°56'S, 114°40'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sighted and exchanged recognition calls with the southbound USS Flasher (T/Cdr. G.W. Grider, USN). (3)
30 Dec 1944 While making the northbound passage of Lombok Strait, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) is detected by enemy patrol vessels and fired upon.
After diving one of the patrol vessels is attack with two torpedoes which missed.
USS Hardhead was not depth charged and managed to complete the northbound passage of Lombok Strait submerged.
2030 H/30 - Commenced the northbound transit of Lombok Strait on the surface.

2112 H/30 - In position 08°25'S, 115°50'E, sight and radar contact was obtained on two small and one medium patrol boats stationed just north of the southern entrance to Lombok Strait. They were evenly spaced across the Strait. The largest patrol boat was in a sector of reduced visibility in the west. They were evaded at 18 knots.

2250 H/30 - In position 08°18'S, 115°52'E, sight and radar contact was obtained on two large patrol boats at 13000 yards bearing 015. They were making heavy smkoke and were on an intercepting course to the south-east. It was believed the earlier patrol boats encountered had reported us. Reversed course and headed for an area with reduced visibility and approaching rain squall off the eastern tip of Bali. The patrol boats detected our course change immediately and continued closing. Tracked them at 18-19 knots.
2256 H/30- When 5000 yards from the shore of Bali the nearer patrol boat fired two rounds from a range of about 7500 yards. They fell short 2000 to 3000 yards.
2307 H/30 - The patrol boat fired three more rounds from 6000 yards just before USS Hardhead entered the rain squall. Course was set to the north-west to clear Bali.
2323 H/30 - Seven depth charges were heard to explode.

2325 H/30 - USS Hardhead was now 2000 yards from the coast of Bali. The rain squall cleared and there was a full moon. Range had closed to 4000 yards. One enemy round ricocheted over the bridge. Gave left full rudder and observed a splash on the starboard bow weere would have been if not for the course change.

2328 H/30 - It had been enough and USS Hardhead dived to radar depth. Paralled targets course hoping the enemy would close for a stern tube shot. The target did not cooperate so changed course to the right and closed his track for a bow tube shot. Went to periscope depth when the range closed to 2700 yards.

2338 H/30 - North-east of the eastern tip of Bali, in position 08°20'S, 115°45'E, fired two bow torpedoes at a large patrol vessel or minesweeper from 2900 yards. Targets speed was 16 knots. The target changed course 90° to the right at about the time the torpedoes should have reached his position so both missed.
2343 H/30 - Sighted the second patrol boat astern of the target.

2345 H/30 - Went deep and rigged for silent running and depth charge attack. Proceeded north-eastwards at 3 to 5 knots. Sound reported two more patrol vessels to join the search party. No depth charges were dropped and the enemy apparently was never in contact, probably because of two density layers Hardhead was hiding under.
0410 H/31 - All clear except for the sound of echo ranging astern. Surfaced 12 miles from the coast. The transit of Lombok Strait having been completed. (15)
4 Jan 1945 At 0530 H/4, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) entered her patrol area in position 06°00'N, 104°30'E. (15)

6 Jan 1945 North-east of Kota Bharu, in position 06°49'N, 102°58'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) attacked a Japanese escort vessel with four torpedoes which all missed. One of the torpedoes broached and ran erratic possibly spoiling the attack.

The escort was one of three out of the frigates Chiburi, Kaibokan 17 and Kaibokan 19 which were escorting the fleet tanker Nichei Maru (10020 GRT, built 1938) from Singapore to Japan. The tanker had been torpedoed and sunk just before by USS Besugo (T/Cdr. T.L. Wogan, USN). [All links to Japanese ships are offsite links.]

1852 H/6 - Received a contact report from USS Besugo that she had sighted a ship bearing 182° at a range of 26000 yards from her own position of 06°30'N, 102°51'E. USS Hardhead changed course and went to flank speed to intercept.
1905 H/6 - Besugo reported course of the enemy as being 325° at 12 knots.
1906 H/6 - Enemy course was now reported to be 000°.
1915 H/6 - Enemy course was now 315°.
1932 H/6 - In position 06°35'N, 102°59'E, obtained radar contact bearing 245° at a range of 24200 yards. Sighted a tanker and one escort through the periscope. Commenced tracking.
1942 H/6 - Reported the contact to USS Besugo. Enemy course was 040°.
2016 H/6 - Reported to Besugo that enemy course was 030° at 13.5 knots. Target was now bearing 233° at 19250 yards. Planned to track in position on starboard bow of target and attack after Besugo.

Received instructions from Besugo to make combined attacks from the port bow. Took course to change position at flank speed. The target group consisted of one escort ahead and one well aft on each quarter of the tanker.

2100 H/6 - In position 15000 yards dead ahead of the target, about 7000 yards off track using 030° as base course. USS Besugo was bearing 254° at a range of 4600 yards. Commenced approach maintaining position with a small port angle on the bow to be in a good firing position if Besugo would be detected and the target would alter course away.
2120 H/6 - Changed course left to present our smallest silhouette to the leading escort which was bearing 155° at 2500 yards.

2121 H/6 - Observed two hits on the target, one in the bow and one amidships. The second hit produced flames which illuminated the vicinity. Besugo was visible at 5000 yards without bninoculars. Made out details of the escort ahead, he was a single stack patrol vessel or minesweeper. The target was a large tanker, which started to sink rapidly by the bow. Ordered to shift target to the leading escort but the Officer of the Deck was blinded by the flames and a setup could not be quickly made.
2122 H/6 - Submerged to radar depth to continue the approach on the selected escort.
2123 H/6 - Only a small fire on the water could be seen through the periscope. The escort could not be made out.

2126 H/6 - Surfaced and changed course for a stern tube shot at the escort. Nothing was visible of the tanker except a tall column of smoke. Besugo was now bearing 240° at 5000 yards. Tracked the escort coming towards at about 13 knots at a range of 3000 yards and still closing. Angle on the bow was 15° starboard.

2130 H/6 - In position 06°49'N, 102°58'E, fired four stern torpedoes from 3500 yards for a torpedo run of 2000 yards. One of the torpedoes was seen to broach and run erratic. One torpedo passed close aboard the target but all missed. No end of run explosions were heard possibly due to the shallow water of only 30 fathoms.
2134 H/6 - The escort came to a halt. Hardhead meanwhile retired to the north-east as did Besugo. (18)

15 Jan 1945 At 1135 H/15, north-east of Pulau Redang, in position 06°04'N, 103°08'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) is strafed by a small Japanese float plane which had not been picked up on the SD radar and had not been seen earlier due to the low clouds. USS Hardhead crash dived. A lookout reported that the bomb racks of this aircraft were empty.
USS Hardhead surfaced at 1306 H/15. (15)

21 Jan 1945 USS Blackfin (T/Lt.Cdr. C.L. Raguet, USN) finally joined the coordinated attack group, which was operating off the Gulf of Siam, which was made up of USS Besugo (T/Cdr. T.L. Wogan, USN), USS Blackfin (T/Lt.Cdr. C.L. Raguet, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN). (15)
24 Jan 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) tried but failed to attack the damaged Japanese tanker Sarawak Maru (5135 GRT, built 1943).
The tanker had been damaged earlier in the day by USS Besugo (T/Cdr. T.L. Wogan, USN).

The tanker was en-route from Yulin, Hainan to Singapore and had been escorted by the destroyer Shigure, which had been torpedoes and sunk even before the tanker was damaged by USS Blackfin (T/Lt.Cdr. C.L. Raguet, USN), and the frigates Kanju, Miyake and Kaibokan 13.

0540 H/24 - Received a contact report from USS Blackfin (T/Lt.Cdr. C.L. Raguet, USN) that she had radar contact on a convoy bearing 345° at a range of 16500 yards from position 06°07'N, 103°48'E at 0510 H/24. Enemy course was 200° at 14 knots.
0559 H/24 - Received a message from Blackfin giving the position at 0550 H/24 as 06°00'N, 103°47'E. Enemy course 180° at 13 knots. Blackfin was going to attack on the surface.

0615 H/24 - Received a contact report from USS Besugo (T/Cdr. T.L. Wogan, USN) that she had radar contact on bearing 322° at a range of 12500 yards. The position Besugo transmitted was received garbled.
0620 H/24 - Went to flank speed on an interception course.
0641 H/24 - Sighted a flash over the horizon bearing 055°. This was thought to have been the result of an attack by USS Blackfish [which was correct].
0644 H/24 - Besugo reported she was diving to make an attack.
0715 H/24 - Sighted a large tanker bearing 055° at a range of about 24000 yards. Position was 05°53'N, 103°30'E.
0717 H/24 - Observed and felt a torpedo hit or hits on the tanker. Visibility was poor though. This was thought to have been the result of the attack by USS Besugo [which was indeed correct].
0745 H/24 - The tanker was seen to be milling around and looked down by the bow.

0752 H/24 - Sighted the masts of two escort vessels in the vicinity of the tanker. They moved off to the westward and the tanker got underway on a base course of 160° at speeds between 5 to 8 knots. Hardhead was trailing the enemy from ahead to determine the base course and maintain contact until USS Besugo and / or USS Blackfin reported being in contact.
0843 H/24 - USS Blackfin reported that tanker damaged and one escort vessel sunk out of a total of five ships. The convoy thus consisted of the tanker and four escorts.

0920 H/24 - Reported to USS Besugo and USS Blackfin that USS Hardhead was in contact with the tanker and two escorts bearing 015° at a range of 22000 yards. Enemy course 180° at 6 knots. Hardhead trailing from ahead.
0922 H/24 - Blackfin reported being in contact with the tanker and two escorts bearing 240° at a range of 24000 yards from position 06°00'N, 103°50'E.
0954 H/24 - Dived to attack from ahead after having reported of doing so to the other two submarines four minutes earlier.

The tanker had tracked on a base course of 155° to 160° at speeds of 10 to 12 knots. At times the base course appeared to be 140°. Course 160° was the direct route towards Singapore which most likely would be the destination.

1030 H/24 - The tanker changes it's base course to about 190° and increased speed to 13 to 15 knots. Range was 14000 yards. Hardhead closed at standard to full submerged speed for fourty minutes but could not close the range to under 7000 yards.

1057 H/24 - Sound picked up light screws bearing 335°. Sighted a large patrol vessel on the same bearing at a range of about 5600 yards. Angle on the bow was 20° port. Enemy course was 175°. Position was now 05°36'N, 103°44'E.
1116 H/24 - Shifted target from the tanker to the escort vessel first seen at 1057 hours.
1120 H/24 - Flooded up all torpedo tubes. Set torpedo depths to four feet.
1125 H/24 - The escort hoisted several signals, speeded up and passed at 3000 yards with a 90° port angle on the bow.
1135 H/24 - An aircraft was seen to be patrolling over the tanker.
1140 H/24 - Opened the range to the target group to surface to make an end around.
1253 H/24 - Surfaced. The target group was not in sight. Chased at flank speed.

1435 H/24 - USS Blackfin surfaced bearing 315° at a range of about 4 miles. She reported that the target passed Pulau Tenggol at 1330 hours while making 16 knots and that she had been forced down by an aircraft. Discontinued the chase. Set course to return to the position of the attack by USS Besugo.
1500 H/24 - Blackfin, which was to perform lifeguard durties, set course for her assigned lifeguard station.
1828 H/24 - In position 05°31'N, 103°41'E, sighted the tops of two escort vessels bearing 015° at a range of about 9 miles.
1840 H/24 - Blackfin reported contact on two ships bearing 305° at a range of 11 miles from position 05°41'N, 103°55'E.
1857 H/24 - Hardhead informed Blackfin that the contact appeared to be two escorts.
1913 H/24 - Blackfin reported that the ships were estimated to be good targets and considered to be a transport and a patrol vessel.

1946 H/24 - Hardhead informed Blackfin that the westernmost enemy vessel was definitely a patrol vessel. Blackfin subsequently also identified both vessels to be patrol vessels and stopped her approach.
2047 H/24 - Received patrol orders from USS Besugo which also reported to have obtained three hits on the tanker. (15)

26 Jan 1945 At 0757 H/26, in position 05°48'N, 104°41'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sighted a Japanese frigate bearing 027° at a range of about 18000 yards. The target was tracked on a base course of 215° at speeds between 13 and 15 knots. Hardhead was trailing from ahead.
At 0815 H/26, a contact report was sent to USS Besugo (T/Cdr. T.L. Wogan, USN).
At 0945 H/26, the base course of the enemy vessel was determined to be 180° at 14 knots.
At 1030 H/26, USS Besugo was sighted bearing 060° at a range of about 5 miles.
At 1041 H/26, USS Hardhead informed USS Besugo that she was diving to make an attack.
At 1114 H/26, the target changed it base course to the right by about 15°.
At 1130 H/26, all torpedo tubes were flooded up.

At 1132 H/26, the target passed USS Hardhead at a range of 2400 yards with a 100° port angle on the bow. No torpedoes were fired due to the smooth sea and excessive run during the torpedoes would almost certainly be seen and evaded. T/Cdr. Greenup took pictures of the target through the periscope.
At 1231 H/26, USS Hardhead surfaced and commenced an end around.
At 1253 H/26, USS Besugo signalled she expected to be able to intercept and attack the enemy around 1630 hours.
At 1530 H/26, the target was lost from sight due to the decreasing visibility and the enemy was not seen again. (15)

30 Jan 1945 On completion of the lifeguard duties, USS Blackfin (T/Lt.Cdr. C.L. Raguet, USN) reported the was rejoining the coordinated attack group which was further made up of USS Besugo (T/Cdr. T.L. Wogan, USN), USS Blackfin (T/Lt.Cdr. C.L. Raguet, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN). (15)

1 Feb 1945 Late in the evening, USS Blackfin (T/Lt.Cdr. C.L. Raguet, USN) left the coordinated attack group which was further made up of USS Besugo (T/Cdr. T.L. Wogan, USN), USS Blackfin (T/Lt.Cdr. C.L. Raguet, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN). USS Blackfin had been ordered to patrol off the western entrance to the Pelawan Passage. (19)

2 Feb 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) torpedoed and sank the Japanese small tanker Nanshin Maru No.19 (834 GRT, built 1944) off the east coast of Malaya, south-east of Pulau Redang, in position 05°40'N, 103°17'E.
2341 H/1 - USS Besugo (T/Cdr. T.L. Wogan, USN) reported radar contact bearing 015° at a range of 15000 yards from position 05°04'N, 104°28'E.
2346 H/1 - Set course to intercept at flank speed.
2348 H/1 - Besugo reported enemy course about 225°, speed 15 knots.
2355 H/1 - Besugo reported enemy course now 200°.
0016 H/1 - USS Blackfin (T/Lt.Cdr. C.L. Raguet, USN) reported her position course and speed upon requist from USS Besugo.
0030 H/1 - Reported our position to Besugo. Plot showed Blackfin and Hardhead would intercept around the same time.
0036 H/1 - Obtained SJ radar and sight contact on USS Blackfin bearing 060° at a range of 9300 yards.
0046 H/1 - Besugo reported the targets position again. The target group was made up of four ships. It was believed a cruiser or destroyer was present.
0052 H/2 - Blackfin report having Hardhead in sight bearing 225°.
0110 H/2 - Besugo reported enemy speed 13 knots.
0129 H/2 - Besugo reported enemy course now 180°.
0149 H/2 - Obtained radar and sight contact on the target group consisting of a medium sized ship and two escorted on bearing 052° at a range of 12800 yards from position 04°30'N, 104°22'E.
0152 H/2 - USS Blackfin reported also being in contact.
0157 H/2 - USS Besugo dived to make an attack run. Hardhead was trailing from ahead on the port bow and Blackfin also from ahead but on the starboard bow.
0229 H/2 - Observed torpedo hit or hits on the target. A terrific fire developed with quite a fireworks display. The fire quickly spread throughout the length of the target.
0230 H/2 - Heard a loud explosion and the fires increased in intensity.
0232 H/2 - Heard four explosions, thought to be depth charges.
0300 H/2 - The last of the fire on the water disappeared.
0310 H/2 - Closed the scene and contacted a single escort east of the position of the sinking, searching the vicinity and slowly moving westward.
0351 H/2 - Blackfin reported two escorts and target bearing 065°, range 13000 yards from position 04°30'N, 104°21'E.
0357 H/2 - Blackfin reported she was diving to deliver an attack run.

Hardhead closed the position of Blackfin's contact and contacted two escorts which were alternately signalling and dropping depth charges. Remained in the vicinity south of the escorts to await the developments of Blackfin's attack.
0441 H/2 - Received instructions from Besugo to take up a new patrol position. Course was set accordingly.
0443 H/2 - Heard 14 depth charges exploding.
0500 H/2 - Besugo reported the results of her attack as one hit on a probable destroyer.
0541 H/2 - Received new patrol instructions from Besugo.
0601 H/2 - Heard 7 depth charges exploding.
1248 H/2 - Blackfin reported having sighted only escort vessels and that she had not carried though her attack because of that.
1950 H/2 - Received instructions to patrol about 10 miles from Pulau Redang during the night with Besuga 20 miles to the eastwards of Hardhead.
2132 H/2 - In position 05°45'N, 103°28'E, obtained radar contact bearing 311° at a range of 13500 yards. Commenced tracking.
2147 H/2 - Sent contact reported to Besugo. Targets course was 240° at 6 knots.
2154 H/2 - Reported target on course 220° at 10 knots to Besugo. Closed to 9000 yards and determined the contact to be a single ship.
2209 H/2 - Sent an amplifying contact report to Besugo.
2214 H/2 - Besugo reported course and speed.
2232 H/2 - Besugo reported her position as 25 miles east of Hardhead.

2255 H/2 - T/Cdr. Greenup decided to attack before Besugo arrived. Moon will rise at 2311 and Hardhead's position was five miles from the rocks to the south-east of Pulau Redang in 30 fathoms of water.
2310 H/2 - In position 05°40'N, 103°17'E, fired four bow torpedoes from 1650 yards for a torpedo run of 1500 yards at a small tanker or transport with engines aft.

2311 H/2 - A torpedo hit was seen amidships followed by a second torpedo hit amidships shortly afterwards. The target broke in two and by the time the smoke had cleared one minute later only the bow and stern were visible. These sank rapidly. Searched the vicinity but did not find any survivors.
2316 H/2 - Reported the results of the attack to USS Besugo.
2335 H/2 - Received now patrol orders from USS Besugo.
3 Feb 1945 Around 1400 H/3, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) left patrol to return to Fremantle. (15)
4 Feb 1945 At 1608 H/4, in position 00°02'S, 106°54'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sighted and exchanged calls with the northbound USS Bergall (T/Cdr. J.M. Hyde, USN).
At 1646 H/4, a signal was received assigning USS Hardhead to lifeguard duties off Singapore for a B-29 strike on 6 February.

At 1818 H/4, in position 00°23'S, 107°10'E, USS Hardhead sighted an unidentified British submarine. This was HMS Thule (A/Lt.Cdr. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, DSC, RN) which reported seeing a mast to the south-west at the same time. It was thought to be the mast of a war vessel. Perhaps this was Hardhead's periscope. (20)

5 Feb 1945 At 0017 H, in position 00°17'N, 106°27'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sighted and exchanged calls with the southbound USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN). [Nothing is mentioned on this in the patrol report of USS Kraken.]

At 1729 H/5, a signal was received which started that the air strikes scheduled for the next day were cancelled. USS Hardhard set course for Karimata Strait to proceed to Fremantle. Later it was decided to call at Onslow before proceeding to Fremantle due to fuel shortage. (15)
6 Feb 1945 At 1740 H/6, in position 01°41'S, 108°21'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sighted and exchanged calls with the southbound USS Perch (T/Cdr. B.C. Hills, USN). (15)
8 Feb 1945 At 1833 H/8, in position 05°13'S, 112°39'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sighted and exchanged calls with the eastbound USS Besugo (T/Cdr. T.L. Wogan, USN). (15)
9 Feb 1945 At 0200 H/9, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) commenced the southbound transit of Lombok Strait.

At 0200 H/9, in position 08°33'S, 115°48'E, obtained radar contacts on a small patrol boat in the northern passage and a small patrol boat in the southern passage. Evaded them on the surface without being detected.
At 0430 H/9, USS Hardhead completed the southbound transit of Lombok Strait.

At 2355 H/9, in position 13°14'S, 114°54'E, calls were exchanged with the SJ radar with the northbound USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN). While ettempting to pass information on the conditions in Lombok Strait contact was lost. (15)
10 Feb 1945 At 0225 H/10, in position 13°49'S, 114°47'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) exchanged calls by SJ radar with the southbound USS Perch (T/Cdr. B.C. Hills, USN). (15)
11 Feb 1945 Around 1430 H/11, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) arrived at Onslow. She was unable to fuel on this day due to damage to the dock fender. (15)
12 Feb 1945 Around 1400 H/12, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) departed Onslow for Fremantle. (15)
15 Feb 1945 Around 0700 H/15, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) ended her 3rd war patrol at Fremantle.
At Fremantle she was taken in hand for refit by Submarine Division 181 and USS Euryale (T/Cdr. R.R. Gurley, USN). (21)
12 Mar 1945 From 12 to 17 March 1945, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) conducted a training period off Fremantle. (22)

15 Mar 1945 During 15 and 16 March 1945, USS Gabilan (T/Cdr. W.B. Parham, USN), USS Besugo (T/Lt.Cdr. H.E. Miller, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) conducted exercises off Fremantle with HMAS Bunbury (Lt. J.F. Bleckman, RANR(S)) and HMAS Kiama (T/Lt. S.J. Benson, RANR(S)). (23)

20 Mar 1945 Around 1300 H/20, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) departed from Fremantle for her 4th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Siam and to conduct a special (minelaying) operation.

Before proceeding on patrol exercises were carried out with USS Gabilan (T/Cdr. W.B. Parham, USN), USS Besugo (T/Lt.Cdr. H.E. Miller, USN), USS Corpus Christie (Cdr. W.W. Childress, USCG), USS Hutchinson (Lt.Cdr. E.H. Houghtaling, USCG) and HMAS Kiama (T/Lt. S.J. Benson, RANR(S)).
On completion of the exercises around 2300 H/20, USS Gabilan and USS Hardhead proceeded on patrol. (24)
23 Mar 1945 At 0800 H/23, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) arrived at Onslow to top off with fuel and to embark Rear Admiral J. Fife, USN, the Commander Submarines, Seventh Fleet.
At 1105 H/23, USS Hardhead got underway for her patrol area.
Once again USS Gabilan (T/Cdr. W.B. Parham, USN) was in company and during the passage northward to the Lombok Strait the two submarines carried out exercises with each other. (22)

25 Mar 1945 At 1305 H/25, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN, with T/R.Adm. J. Fife, USN on board) sighted the periscope shears of two submarines. This turned out to be the southbound USS Perch (T/Cdr. B.C. Hills, USN) and northbound USS Croaker (T/Cdr. W.B. Thomas, USN).

At 1535 H/25, USS Hardhead delivered a bag of mail to USS Perch which in turn informed Hardhead that she had encountered two small patrol boats in Lombok Strait and that she had been strafed by an enemy fighter plane 100 miles south of Lombok that morning. This information was passed to USS Gabilan (T/Cdr. W.B. Parham, USN) and USS Croaker. This last submarine had already been informed of this by USS Perch though when they had encountered each other. (22)
26 Mar 1945 At 0015 H/26, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN, with T/R.Adm. J. Fife, USN on board) commenced the northbound passage of Lombok Strait on the surface.
At 0043 H/26, in position 08.23'S, 115.49'E, sighted and obtained radar contact on two small craft at a range of about 8500 yards. Avoided them to the westward.
At 0310 H/26, USS Hardhead completed the transit of Lombok Strait. She then set course to pass to the west of Kangean Island. (22)

31 Mar 1945 At 2257 H/31, in position 01°51'N, 107°42'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN, with T/R.Adm. J. Fife, USN on board) made rendezvous with HMS Tudor (Lt. S.A. Porter, DSC, RN) to transfer mail and fresh meat.
HMS Tudor informed HMS Hardhead that in the morning, she had sighted a periscope, believed to be from an enemy submarine 10 miles north-east of the rendezvous position. (22)
2 Apr 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN, with T/R.Adm. J. Fife, USN on board) lays mines off Cape Camau, French Indo-China. On completion course was set to patrol in the Gulf of Siam.
[No further details available. These should be a separate report on the laying of the minefield but so far we have not been able to find it.]

6 Apr 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN, with T/R.Adm. J. Fife, USN on board) torpedoed and sank the Japanese transport Araosan Maru (6886 GRT, built 1944) in the Gulf of Siam in position 09°37'N, 102°48'E. Aroasan Maru was an engine aft ship and looked like a tanker.

2032 I/5 - Sighted three ships bearing 354° from position 09°09'N, 103°15'E, range 12 miles. The target group consisted of a large tanker, a small freighter and a small trawler. At first it was believed that the trawler was an escort since visibility was poor due to the evening twilight. Commenced tracking and stayed clear until it was completely dark.

2053 I/5 - Obtained radar contact bearing 351° at 19600 yards. The target group was on course 250° at 10 knots. Two freighters were apparently keeping station on the tanker but they moved around so radically that the radar operator had difficulty staying on the tanker. The tanker pip was in two parts possibly because of the large bridge stucture forward and this increased tracking difficulties.

2152 I/5 - In position 09°19'N, 103°01'E, fired six bow torpedoes at a large, empty tanker of about 10000 tons from 2800 yards for a torpedo run of 2500 yards. One torpedo hit forward of the bridge. The tanker fired several rounds from a medium caliber bow gun and there was also much automatic weapon fire. All well clear of Hardhead.
2155 I/5 - Commenced firing two stern torpedoes at a freighter of about 2500 tons from 2100 yards for a torpedo run of 2000 yards. Both missed.
The tanker was seen to settle by the bow and slowed down but soon increased speed again to 7 to 8 knots and started an erratic zig zag on a base course of 320° in the general direction of Pulau Wai.

2304 I/5 - In position 09°21'N, 102°56'E, fired three bow torpedoes at the tanker from 2400 yards for a torpedo run of 2000 yards. It was intended to fire for but after firing the third torpedo the setup was found to be in error and all missed.
2307 I/5 - Commenced firing the last two torpedoes aft at the tanker from 1900 yards for a torpedo run of 2000 yards. Both missed.
2337 I/5 - Submerged to radar depth 5000 yards ahead of the tanker for a close shot with the last three torpedoes which were forward.
2352 I/5 - In position 09°21'N, 102°28'E fired one torpedo from 500 yards for a torpedo run of 400 yards. It missed.
0018 I/6 - Surfaced 3000 yards astern of the target group and commenced an end around at a range of 4000 to 5000 yards.

0159 I/6 - An unfavourable zig placed Hardhead dead ahead off the tanker at 2100 yards. All ahead flank and right full rudder. Closed to 1400 yards but was not sighted. All surface running had been with the submarine flooded down since the first attack. The tanker zigged right.

0213 I/6 - In position 09°37'N, 102°48'E fired the last two torpedoes from the bow tubes at a range of 1500 yards for a torpedo run of 1300 yards. The first hit below the stack and the second hit below the bridge.
0217 I/6 - The tanker sank followed by heavy explosions. Hauled clear and made preparations to attack the two freighters with the gun after moonrise.

0258 I/6 - With the trawler in the moon slick, shortly after moonrise, fired 14 rounds of 4" without hitting. Range was 4500 to 3500 yards during the engagement. The trawler returned an ineffective fire with a medium calibre gun and automatic weapons. Position was 09°37'N, 102°48'E.

0340 I/6 - Fired 4 rounds of 4" at the freighter from 3000 yards but the background of Pulau Wai made sighting the results difficult and no hits were obtained. Everytime a shell passed over the target he fired all guns in all directions.
Hauled clear and set course to clear the area before dawn.
0500 I/6 - Send a signal reporting the results of the torpedo attack and also to cancel the planned rendezvous with HMS Tradewind (Lt. J. Nash, DSC, RN).
1757 I/6 - Transmitted a signal reporting the negative results of the gunshoot. Proceeded to Subic Bay to reload with torpedoes and some minor repairs before resuming patrol.

8 Apr 1945 At 0439 I/8, in position 11°26'N, 110°12'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN, with T/R.Adm. J. Fife, USN on board) made rendezvous with USS Rock (T/Cdr. R.A. Keating, Jr., USN) to transfer mail.
At 0602 I/8, calls were exchanged with USS Bashaw (Lt.Cdr. H.S. Simpson, USNR) with the SJ radar. (22)

9 Apr 1945 At 0145 I/9 in position 11°11'N, 109°49'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN, with T/R.Adm. J. Fife, USN on board) made rendezvous with USS Boarfish (T/Cdr. R.L. Gross, USN) to transfer mail. (22)

11 Apr 1945 Around 1230 I/11, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN, with T/R.Adm. J. Fife, USN on board) enters Subic Bay in the Philippines to reload torpedoes, stores and fuel. She was also taken in hand for some repairs by USS Anthedon (T/Capt. R.E. Hawes, USN).
USS Hardhead was escorted in by USS Jaccard (Lt.Cdr. C.R. Hamilton, USNR) which had joined at 0715 I/11.
At Subic Bay, T/R.Adm. Fife left the submarine.
15 Apr 1945 Around 0200 I/15, USS Becuna (T/Cdr. H.D. Sturr, USN) departed from Subic Bay for her 4th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the South China Sea and Gulf of Siam.
She, and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) which departed Subic Bay to resume her 4th war patrol, were escorted out until around 0630 I/15 by USS Eichenberger (T/Lt.Cdr. N. Harrell, USN).

16 Apr 1945 At 2010 I/16, in position 12°05'N, 111°26'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sighted the lights of a hospital ship bearing 340° at a range of 30000 yards according to the SJ radar. Targets course was 030° at 14 knots. Closed the range and definitely identified the target as a properly lighted hospital ship. Radar contact was lost at 32400 yards.
This may have been the Kazuura Maru (6804 GRT, built 1938) (offsite link) which was on passage from Singapore to Hong Kong. (25)
22 Apr 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sank two small Japanese '; sea trucks' with gunfire in the Gulf of Siam in position 10°25'N, 99°24'E.
1145 I/22 - Sighted two sea trucks bearing 278° at 14000 yards range from position 10°20'N, 99°30'E. The target were on a course of 060° at a speed of about 5 knots.
1233 I/22 - Submerged to close and investigate the targets. Only one small machine gun was subsequently sighted on each sea truck.

1352 I/22 - Battle surfaced and engaged both sea trucks with 4", 20mm and .50 caliber at ranges between 4000 and 1200 yards. One sea truck returned fire ineffectively with a small machine gun. Both target caught fire and were abandoned by their crews.

1412 I/22 - Both targets were buring with tall columns of black smoke rising against the hills of Siam and Burma in the background. Cleared the area for deeper water at flank speed without attempting to puck up survivors since the depth of the water was only 12 fathoms. Both sea trucks, of about 150 tons, were observed to sink while clearing the area.

24 Apr 1945 At 0217 I/24, in the Gulf of Siam, in position 07°42'N, 102°32'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) obtained radar contact on bearing 215° at a range of 22000 yards. The SJ radar reported a very small contact. Hardhead closed at flank speed to investigate.

At 0234 I/24, two small pips were seen on the radar screen. They were the same size, one 2000 yards on the beam of the other. They were sighted from the bridge at a range of 14000 yards. Visibility was excellend with first quarter moon. The targets were on course 225° at 10 knots. When the range had closed to 12000 yards it became apparent that the enemy had locked radar on the submarine. The right flank ship slowed and changed course and presented it's beam. It was identified as a medium size escort, probably a minesweeper. The other ship appeared to be a bit smaller. Decided the target group was on an A/S sweep so broke off the contact. (22)
26 Apr 1945 At 0737 I/26, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) left her current patrol area off the east coast of Malaya to patrol off Great Natuna Island. (22)

29 Apr 1945 At 2200 I/29, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) made rendezvous with USS Becuna (T/Cdr. H.D. Sturr, USN) and received plans for a joint search of the Burong Islands from May 1st. (22)
2 May 1945 At 1520 I/2, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) set course to the north to patrol once more off Great Natuna Island. (22)
5 May 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) is ordered to leave patrol in the evening of 6 May and proceed to Fremantle. (22)

6 May 1945 At 1705 I/6, north of the Riau Islands, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) made rendezvous with USS Baya (T/Lt.Cdr. B.C. Jarvis, USN). USS Hardhead received mail for her crew from USS Baya.
On completion of the transfer USS Hardhead set course for Karimata Strait to proceed to Fremantle. (22)
7 May 1945 In Karimata Strait USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) encountered and exchanged calls with the SJ radar with two northbound US submarines.
At 2256 I/7, calls were exchanged with USS Blower (T/Cdr. J.H. Campbell, USN) and at 2328 H/7 with USS Perch (T/Lt.Cdr. C.D. McCall, USN). (22)
9 May 1945 At 2030 I/9, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) commenced the southbound transit of Lombok Strait on the surface.
At 2255 I/9, the transit of Lombok Strait was completed. There was no sign of the enemy during the transit. (22)
10 May 1945 At 0948 I/10, in position 11°10'S, 115°20'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) exchanged calls and information with the northbound USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN).
At 1950 I/10, in position 13°18'S, 115°00'E, USS Hawkbill exchanged calls with the northbound HMS Trump (Lt. A.A. Catlow, RN). (26)
