11 May 1945 At 1415 I/11, in approximate position 17°20'S, 114°06'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) exchanged calls with the northbound HMS Tudor (Lt. S.A. Porter, DSC, RN). (27)
12 May 1945 At 0925 I/12, arrived at Exmouth Gulf to embark some fuel and stores.
She departed for Fremantle around 1600 I/12.
At 1730 I/12, gunnery exercises (4" and 20mm) were carried out on the wreck off the entrance to Exmouth Gulf. (22)
15 May 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) ended her 4th war patrol at Fremantle.
She had anchored in Gage Roads the previous evening entering harbour the following day.
At Fremantle she was taken in hand for refit by a relief crew and the submarine repair unit. (22)
31 May 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) commenced a training period off Fremantle. (28)
1 Jun 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) is docked at Fremantle to have the starboard propeller changed as it was noisy. (28)
2 Jun 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) is undocked and continued with her training programme. (28)
9 Jun 1945 Around 1400 I/9, (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) departed from Fremantle for her 5th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Java Sea. (28)

10 Jun 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) encountered heavy weather conditions during her northbound passage. Sea conditions are such that she can proceed only at one engine speed. Some minor damage was sustained which could quickly be repaired.
Conditions remained the same during the night but improved a bit around 0600 I/11 when it was found possible to proceed at two engine speed. (28)
12 Jun 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) suffers a defect to the water distilling unit which was now out of commission.

At 1530 I/12, rendezvous was made with the southbound USS Blueback (Cdr. M.K. Clementson, USN) which provided information on Lombok Strait and the patrol area and also transferred a spare part. Repairs could now be undertaken. (28)
14 Jun 1945 At 0126 I/14, in position 14°25'S, 114°40'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) exchanged calls with the southbound USS Cobia (T/Cdr. A.L. Becker, USN). (28)

14 Jun 1945 At 2023 I/14, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) broke off her northward passage and set course to proceed to Onslow to make repairs to the distiller unit which had so far proven unsuccessful.
It had been the intention to pass Lombok Strait northbound this night if repairs had been successful. (28)
16 Jun 1945 At 1000 I/16, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) arrived at Onslow for repairs to the distilling unit. Also she topped off with fuel.
At the spare parts had to be flown in from Fremantle she had to wait until there arrived and the repairs were completed before she could resume her 5th war patrol. (28)
19 Jun 1945 At 1600 I/16, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) departed Onslow to resume her 5th war patrol. The spare parts having arrived the previous day by RAAF aircraft. (28)

20 Jun 1945 At 1800 I/20, in position 15°54'S, 114°26'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) made rendezvous with the southbound HMS Thorough (Lt. A.G. Chandler, RNR) receive information of Lombok Strait and the Java Sea area. (28)

21 Jun 1945 At 2300 I/21, just south of the southern entrance to Lombok Strait, in position 08°55'S, 115°44'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) exchanged calls with the SJ radar with the southbound USS Caiman (Lt.Cdr. W.L. Fey, Jr., USN). (28)

21 Jun 1945 At 2320 I/21, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN), having just commenced the northbound passage of Lombok Strait obtained radar and sight contact bearing 006° at a range of 5600 yards. This was thought to be HMS Taciturn (Lt.Cdr. E.T. Stanley, DSO, DSC, RN). Evasive action was taken and the other submarine submerged before recognition could be initiated.
This was indeed HMS Taciturn which indeed dived on seeing another submarine which was thought to be USS Hardhead. (29)

23 Jun 1945 While operating in the Java Sea, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) torpedoed and sank the Japanese auxiliary submarine chaser Cha 113 (former Dutch minesweeper HrMs A) in position 05°50'S, 114°18'E.
She then sank the Shuttle Boat No. 833 (200 GRT) with gunfire in position 06°04'S, 114°21'E and the and auxliary submarine chaser Cha 42 with gunfire in position 05°39'S, 114°19'E.
0226 I/23 - Completed the northbound passage of Lombok Strait.

1552 I/23 - north-east of Madura Island, in position 06°38'S, 114°20'E, sighted three small ships through the periscope bearing 247° at a range of about 15000 yards. The contact consisted of a large 'Sugar Charlie', a medium coastal minelayer and a submarine chaser on a northerly course.

T/Cdr. Greenup was determined to sink all three but if Hardhead was detected during a day approach it was feared that all three might be able to get away. So it was decided to gain a position ahead first.
1650 I/22 - Submerged to have a good look at the target group. All were zigging radically with escorts patrolling station.
T/Cdr. Greenup decided to attack after dark with gun or torpedoes using the new ST radar equipped periscope for a close, accurate submerged shot.

1938 I/22 - Surfaced and manoeuvred to gain a position down moon of the target group. The submarine chaser was on the port flank and the coastal minelayer was on the staboard flank of the 'Sugar Charlie'.
2115 I/22 - Escorts have fallen back on each quarter of the 'Sugar Charlie'. Decided to attack the 'Sugar Charlie' with gunfire hoping for an early hit.

2127 I/22 - Opened fire with the 5" gun from 4000 yards. Fired four shots which were very close but did not hit. The gun misfired on firing the fifth round and the escorts return fire became rather accurate. Broke off the action at a range of 2500 yards. This was very disappointing.
Each time Hardhead now closed to under 7500 yards she was taken under fire by the escorts. Hardhead now retired to gain a position ahead and wait for moonset to try another surface approach.

0111 I/23 - Change of plans. Submerged for a torpedo attack. Both escorts were now on the port flank of the target. Decided to attack the coastal minelayer with the bow tubes the 'Sugar Charlie' with the stern tubes.
0133 I/23 - In position 05°50'S, 114°18'E, fired four bow torpedoes from 1200 yards for a 1150 yards torpedo run. Target was the auxiliary minelayer.
0134 I/23 - One hit in the bow of the minelayer was obtained which was seen to settle rapidly.

0140 I/23 - Fired two stern torpedoes (three were intended) at the 'Sugar Charlie' from 1000 yards for a 900 yards torpedo run. The first torpedo fired broached and circled to the left. The target saw the torpedo and turned left sharply. Firing was stopped after the second torpedo had been fired.
0144 I/23 - Observed the minelayer to sink. The 'Sugar Charlie' and the auxiliary submarine chaser were milling around.

0205 I/23 - The submarine chaser passed 500 yards down the starboard side. Rigged for silent running. The target group subsequently headed south and then north again now with the submarine chaser in the lead.
0213 I/23 - Fired three stern torpedoes at the 'Sugar Charlie' from 1200 yards for a 1100 yards torpedo run. No hits were obtained. All stern torpedoes had now been expended.
0243 I/23 - Surfaced.
0259 I/23 - Manned all guns.
0305 I/23 - Opened fire on the 'Sugar Charlie' from his starboard quarter with the submarine chaser 5000 to 7000 yards on the port bow of the target and making no attempt to interfere.

0315 I/23 - The target was ablaze from stem to stern. Closed the range to 1500 yards to afford automatic weapons a chance to practice. Two hours later, after burning intensely, the target was seen to sink.
0327 I/23 - Opened fire on the submarine chaser from about 5000 yards. The target was manoeuvring radically and was difficult to see clearly in the gun scopes.
0328 I/23 - The target was seen to jettison several depth charges.

0339 I/23 - 25mm gunfire from the submarine chaser was getting too close when the range was closed to 4000 yards so hauled clear to obtain a better position eastward and down moon of the target. The target chased at 12 knots, for several minutes and sprayed our area rather accurately with his 25mm gunfire. As no.3 main engine was out of action due to a defect Hardhead's five knot speed advantage opened the range very slowly.

0415 I/23 - Resumed fire. Closed the range to 2500 yards and started hitting with the 5" gun. The target was blazing but was still making full speed in a tight circle. Everytime he came around to our side of the circle all guns would pepper the enemy from only 300 yards. 5" finally stopped him dead in the water from a range of 500 yards. The action was broken off when there were only six rounds of 5" remaining. The submarine chaser was destroyed although the wreckage was not seen to sink before Hardhead cleared the area.
23 Jun 1945 At 1800 I/23, in position 05°44'S, 113°52'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) and USS Bullhead (T/Cdr. W.T. Griffith, USN) made rendezvous.
Plans were made to both search between Banjarmasin, Borneo and Bawean Island as a coordinated attack group. (28)

24 Jun 1945 At 1611 I/24, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) received a contact report from USS Bullhead (T/Cdr. W.T. Griffith, USN); Ship sighted bearing 352°, 14 miles from position 05°51'S, 113°36'E. Enemy course 055°. Hardhead set course to intercept.
At 1642 I/24, USS Bullhead was sighted bearing 214° and smoke on bearing 279°.
At 1658 I/24, USS Hardhead was in position to attack so requisted permission from USS Bullhead to dive and make an attack. One minute later permission was granted.

At 1703 I/24, USS Hardhead dived and commenced a two hour and 11 minute approach on the target which turned out to be a hospital ship. When the range had been closed to 5000 yards the target stopped and turned right. A photograph was taken through the periscope. The ship was identified as being the Takasago Maru (9315 GRT, built 1937). This was however not a correct identification as this hospital ship was in Japanese home waters at this time. This must have been the Hikawa Maru No.2 (6076 GRT, built 1927, former Tenno Maru, former Dutch Op Ten Noort) which was on passage from Jakarta to Ambon.
At 1944 I/24, USS Hardhead surfaced and informed USS Bullhead that the target was a hospital ship and the question was asked if it should be trailed. The answer was negative.
At 2151 I/24, USS Hardhead received instructions from USS Bullhead for a joint search off Ambat Roads, Bali the following night. (30)
25 Jun 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sank a schooner with gunfire off north-east Bali in position 08°19'30"S, 115°38'30"E.
2000 I/25 - Commenced approach of Ambat Roads, northern Bali, 1000 to 2000 yards off the beach. USS Bullhead (T/Cdr. W.T. Griffith, USN) was patrolling off the north-east point of Bali.
2018 I/25 - Sighted a small ship off the north-east point of Bali and asked USS Bullhead if she had contact.
2022 I/25 - Contact was confirmed by USS Bullhead. Commenced closing and made out that the contact was a medium sized schooner.

2033 I/25 - Bullhead reported five contacts besides the schooner so she headed down the coast to intercept these. The schooner was westbound and Hardhead passed her on an opposite course. Hardhead turned round running parellel to the schooner at a range of 500 yards.
2055 I/25 - Bullhead ordered Hardhead to 'get that schooner against the beach'.
2100 I/25 - Bullhead was seen to attack and set afire her contact with automatic weapons.

2125 I/25 - In position 08°19'30"S, 115°38'30"E attacked the loaded auxiliary schooner and quickly set her afire. The schooner was estimated to be of about 100 tons when first seen, however she now appeared to be slightly smaller and was now estimated to be of about 80 tons.
2135 I/25 - Commenced a search of the Bali coast 1000 yards from shore between Ambat roads and Lombok Strait.
2230 I/25 - Made rendezvous with Bullhead and reported the results. Plans were made for a further search of the coast of Bali.
2334 I/25 - Observed start of eclipse of the moon. This spoiled the visibility along the coast.
0030 I/26 - Moon 90% eclipsed.
0155 I/26 - Moon emerged from eclipse.
0230 I/26 - Completed search of Bali coast and set course for patrol station off Surabaya. (30)

25 Jun 1945 At 2147 H/25, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) notified USS Bullhead (T/Cdr. W.T. Griffith, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) of her arrival. These submarines now made up a coordinated attack group. Also a rendezvous was arranged but this did not materialise eventually. (31)
30 Jun 1945 At 2000 I/30, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) received orders to perform lifeguard duties off Surabaya during the nights of 1 through 4 July 1945. (28)

2 Jul 1945 At 0300 I/2, north-west of Surabaya, in position 06°31'S, 112°21'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) obtained radar contact bearing 204° at a range of 12500 yards. Target appeared large enough for torpedoes so commenced tracking and gained position ahead.
0340 I/2, Hardhead submerged and commenced approach.
0357 I/2, Flooded the bow tubes. Range was now 4300 yards.
0411 I/2, The target reversed course and commenced opening the range. Secured the bow tubes.
0433 I/2, Surfaced when the range had opened to 8000 yards to see what was going on.
0435 I/2, The target reversed course again and commenced to close. Submerged and continued the approach.

0516 I/2, Range was now 2800 yards. The target now changed course to head for the Surabaya entrance channel. The contact was too small for torpedo fire and it was probably an armed trawler by the looks of his silhouette.

0539 I/2, Surfaced and trailed the enemy at 5000 yards. As there were only six rounds of 5" ammunition left and knowing nothing on the armament of the enemy vessel, broke contact and set course to patrol to the north-east of Surabaya. (28)
5 Jul 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sank a small Japanese vessel ('Sugar Dog') with gunfire west of Kangean Island.

1407 I/5 - Sighted a 'Sugar Dog' heading north from between Goa Goa and Kamudi Islands. Took position between the target and Kangean Island, submerged and waited for the contact to clear the reef. The target stayed very close to the reef until after sunset and then headed north. Observed that the target had a radio antennae.

1942 I/5 - Surfaced in position 07°01'S, 115°00'E. Opened fire after dark with the 5" gun using five round at slow fire at 500 yards but no hits were obtained. Plastered the enemy with automatic weapons but had great difficulty in starting a fire. Tracer illumination showed his sides full of large 20mm holes.

2134 I/5 - The target finally started burning and sank soon afterward. This 'Sugar Dog' was about 125 feet long and is estimated to be about 300 tons. The target had a small Japanese flag painted on her bow. (28)
6 Jul 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) destroyed a small Japanese or Japanese controlled vessel with gunfire off north-east Bali in position 08°19'S, 115°38'E.
1654 I/6 - Off north-east Bali, sighted a loaded 100 foot, 150 tons 'Victor Able' headed east along the coast of Bali. Followed it and kept it in sight.
1947 I/6 - Surfaced to close the vessel.

2037 I/6 - In position 08°19'S, 115°38'E commenced firing with automatic weapons at a range of 500 yards. This one burned brightly from the first burst of 20mm. The target headed for the beach and made it. Continued fire until the target was an inferno from bow to stern. Periodic explosions indicated the vessel was carrying either gasoline or diesel oil in drums.
2048 I/6 - Set course to return to the Surabaya area. (28)

9 Jul 1945 Shortly after midnight, a crewmember of USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) was diagnosed with appendicitis. A signal was sent to CTF 71 reported the results of the recent attacks and requesting instructions regarding the sick crewmember.
At 2000 I/9, USS Hardhead was ordered to proceed to Onslow to land the sick man and also to replenish ammunition. (28)

10 Jul 1945 At 0040 I/10, In the Java Sea, near Bawean Island, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) and USS Loggerhead (Cdr. R.N. Metcalf, USN) made rendezvous and some mail was transferred by USS Loggerhead to USS Hawkbill.
USS Hardhead then left patrol to proceed to Onslow. (28)
11 Jul 1945 At 0216 I/11, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) commenced the southbound passage of Lombok Strait.
At 0402 I/11, USS Hardhard completed the southbound passage of Lombok Strait. No enemy patrol vessels had been encountered. (28)
13 Jul 1945 Around 1315 I/13, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) ended her 5th war patrol at Onslow, Australia.

15 Jul 1945 At 1010 I/15, after having received 100 rounds of 5", 2160 rounds of 20mm and 1750 rounds of .50 cal. as well as various spare parts, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) departed Onslow, Australia to resume her 5th war patrol.
At 2234 I/15, USS Hardhead transmitted a signal to CTF 71 that the Commanding Officer was ill and that she was requisting instructions.
At 0119 I/16, a signal was received with instructions to return to Onslow. (28)
16 Jul 1945 At 1635 I/16, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) arrived at Onslow.
At 1300 I/17, T/Cdr. Greenup, which was ill, was relieved of command by T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN.
At 1430 I/17, a signal was received from the Commander Submarines Seventh Fleet that upon arrival at Onslow the fifth war patrol of USS Hardhead was terminated. (28)
18 Jul 1945 At 0810 I/18, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) departed from Onslow for her 6th war patrol. She was to patrol in the Java Sea. (32)
20 Jul 1945 At 0715 I/20, in position 15°07'S, 114°46'E, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) exchanged visual calls with the southbound HMS Trenchant (Cdr. A.R. Hezlet, DSO, DSC, RN).
At 2350 I/20, in position 14°53'S, 114°53'E, USS Hardhead overtook the northbound HMS Tiptoe (A/Lt.Cdr. R.L. Jay, RN) and HMS Trump (A/Lt.Cdr. A.A. Catlow, RN). (33)
21 Jul 1945 At 0605 I/21, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) exchanged calls with the southbound USS Charr (T/Cdr. F.D. Boyle, USN). (32)
22 Jul 1945 At 2200 I/22, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) commenced the northbound passage of Lombok Strait.
At 0227 I/23, completed the northbound passage of Lombok Strait and arrived in her patrol area. During the passage there had been no sign of the enemy. (32)
23 Jul 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) sank an enemy patrol vessel with gunfire off the north-east coast of Bali in position 08°10'S, 115°29'E.
0257 I/23 - Obtained SJ radar contact on bearing 268° at a range of 6300 yards. This turned out to be a small patrol vessel on course 080° at 5 knots.
0349 I/23 - Went to surface battle stations having manoeuvred into a position one mile off the coast. The target was now bearing 290° (up moon).
0400 I/23 - Opened fire with the 5" gun from 3700 yards. The target turned right so his gun would bear and also commenced firing. His first salvo was short about 500 yards.
0405 I/23 - Salvo number 15 hit the target which resulted in a large explosion and a billow of flane and dark smoke about 300 feet high.
0406 I/23 - Lost radar contact and saw the target sink. Probably it was a lucky hit in her magazine. Position was 08°10'S, 115°29'E.
0407 I/23 - Obtained radar contact on two targets bearing 290° at 8000 yards. Commenced closing.
0415 I/23 - The contacts turned out to be two small native sail boats. Secured from battle stations. (32)
24 Jul 1945 At 0352 I/24, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) and USS Chub (T/Cdr. C.D. Rhymes, Jr., USN) made rendezvous. Patrol instructions were passed from USS Chub to USS Hardhead. (32)

26 Jul 1945 At 2330 I/26, north of Bali, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) obtained radar contact on seven two-masted sail boats bearing 141° at a range of 8000 yards, about one mile from the beach.
At 2335 I/26, the 20mm guns were manned as it was decided to investigate.

At 0001 I/27, USS Hardhead went alongside four of the seven targets. All had natives on board including women and children. Because of the state of the sea it was impossible to put a boarding party on board. No cargo was seen except for some fishing gear. None of the natives spoke English.
At 0025 I/27, it was decided to leave them alone and USS Hardhead proceeded. (32)
27 Jul 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) destroyed a Japanese vessel with gunfire off the north coast of Bali in position 08°09'S, 115°25'E.

2323 I/26 - North of Bali in position 08°07'S, 115°24'E, USS Hardhead obtained SJ contact bearing 218° at a range of 7200 yards. Manoeuvred to put the target up moon and shadow ourselves by the beach.
2351 I/26 - Went to surface battle stations.
0001 I/27 - Had the target in sight bearing 110° at a range of 6650 yards. Enemy course was 110° at 5 knots.

0026 I/27 - Commenced firing 5" gun at a range of 4300 yards. The target turned right and headed for the beach. This is the first time the target could be seen broadside and we were surprised to find that he was large enough for a torpedo attack but that was too late now.
0033 I/27 - The target commenced to return fire. No fall of shot was observed.
0039 I/27 - The target ran aground. At least three 5" shells had hit the target. Position was 08°09'S, 115°25'E.
0048 I/27 - Closed the target and commenced firing 20mm from 1000 yards.

0051 I/27 - The target caught fire as though by spontaneous combustion. In two minutes time he was on fire from stem to stern. He must have had oil on board. High flames were rising from his three deck hatches.
By the light of his own fire his length was determined to be 250 feet. His appearance was similar to a 'Sugar Charlie Love' of 1500 tons.
Judging from the way the target burned it's contraction was probably mainly of wood. The target was heavily camouflaged with greens, including a palm tree growing out of his mast.
0055 I/27 - Secured from battle stations. The target was observed to burn fof 24 hours and was through the periscope the next day to be totally destroyed. (32)
28 Jul 1945 At 1501 I/28, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) sighted four small motor launches. These were trailed submerged.
At 1925 I/28, USS Hardhead surfaced and commenced closing the motor launches which had entered Ambat Roads.

At 1930 I/28, the 20mm and .50 cal guns were manned but on closing the beach to 1000 yards the motor launches could no longer be seen. A few rounds on 20mm were fired in the hope of hitting one of them and thus illuminate the others. No luck though. USS Hardhead should have surfaced 15 minutes earlier to have been successful.
Around 0430 I/29, with the moon up, USS Hardhead closed the position in which the motor launches were seen again but again they could not be located. (32)
30 Jul 1945 At 0810 I/30, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) picked up HE of screws making 80 revs but nothing was sighted. It was thought this may be HMS Stubborn (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN).
At 1624 I/30, a distant explosion was heard.

At 2057 I/30, Obtained SJ radar contact bearing 120° at a range of 12000 yards. Commenced tracking. The contacts base course was 350° at 8 knots. The contact was under constant helm. Decided to wait for moonrise as this may be HMS Stubborn.
At 0008 I/31, USS Hardhead went to battle stations and commenced approach to identify the contact.
At 0015 I/31, HMS Stubborn was asked for her position by radio.
At 0027 I/31, it was seen that the target appeared to be a submarine. The range was 4000 yards and the target was silhouetted by the moon. Hardhead turned left and broke off the approach.
At 0028 I/31, Sound reported a high noise level on the starboard bow which might be a torpedo and avoiding action was taken.
At 0030 I/31, Received the position of HMS Stubborn which was roughly the same as Hardhead's. Commenced to clear the area.
It was later found out that the HE heard at 0810 I/30 was indeed also HMS Stubborn.
The explosion heard at 1624 I/30, was a bomb from a Japanese aircraft which was dropped on HMS Stubborn. It had missed. (34)

31 Jul 1945 At 2314 I/31, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) obtained SJ radar contact bearing 234°. Commenced tracking and continued doing so until the contact was determined to be HMS Stubborn (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) as she passed her position to USS Hardhead. (34)

4 Aug 1945 At 0830 I/4, west of Goa Goa Island, in position 07°10'S, 114°42'E, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) sighted a 100 tons lugger bearing 326° at a range of 6000 yards. Commenced tracking. Enemy course was 080° at 4 knots.
At 0952 I/4, a Jake aircraft (Aichi E13A, reconnaissance seaplane) was seen circling over the target making it impossible to battle surface for gun action.
At 1324 I/4, the aircraft was no longer in sight so it was decided to battle surface.
At 1333 I/4, USS Hardhead battle surfaced and began to chase the enemy vessel. Range was 4500 yards.

At 1336 I/4, an aircraft was seen bearing 150° at a distance of about 10 miles. T/Lt.Cdr. Haines decided not to play with the target as long as an aircraft was about so four minutes later Hardhead submerged.

At 1416 I/4, once again a Jake aircraft was patrolling over the target. At one moment it appeared that the aircraft made an attack run on the periscope so the Officer of the Deck took the submarine deep and took evasive action.

At 1435 I/4, USS Hardhead returned to periscope depth. The target could still be seen bearing 313° at a range of 8000 yards. It was not possible to catch him submerged and at 1500 I/4, the target was lost from sight. (32)
5 Aug 1945 At 2000 I/5, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) left her patrol area and set course for Karimata Strait. (32)

7 Aug 1945 At 1035 I/7, in approximate position 00°05'N, 106°50'E, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) and USS Bumper (T/Cdr. J.W. Williams, Jr., USN) made rendezvous. Mail was transferred from USS Bumper to USS Hardhead and also a can of much needed coffee. (32)

10 Aug 1945 At 0728 I/10, in approximate position 00°05'N, 106°50'E, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) sighted a westbound British submarine bearing 022° at a range of 6 miles. This was believed to be HMS Sea Scout (Lt. J.W. Kelly, RN).
This was indeed correct as the British submarine also reported seeing USS Hardhead around the same time. (35)
11 Aug 1945 At 1100 I/11, USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) ended her 6th war patrol at Subic Bay.
She was escorted in by USS Woodson (Lt.Cdr. A.R. Corlett, USNR) which had joined around 0600 I/11.
At Subic Bay, USS Hardhead was taken in hand for refit by USS Anthedon (T/.Capt. R.E. Hawes, USN).

31 Aug 1945 The submarine tender USS Howard W. Gilmore (T/Cdr. C.A. Johnson, USN) and the submarines USS Puffer (T/Lt.Cdr. C.R. Dwyer, USN), USS Rasher (T/Lt.Cdr. C.D. Nace, USN), USS Raton (T/Lt.Cdr. G.F. Gugliotta, USN), USS Baya (T/Lt.Cdr. B.C. Jarvis, USN), USS Becuna (T/Lt.Cdr. W.J. Bush, USN), USS Caiman (T/Cdr. W.L. Fey, Jr., USN), USS Blenny (T/Lt.Cdr. C.H. Fuller, Jr., USN), USS Blueback (T/Cdr. M.K. Clementson, USN), USS Boarfish (T/Lt.Cdr. E.C. Blonts, Jr., USN), USS Capitaine (T/Lt.Cdr. E.S. Friedrick, USN), USS Carbonero (T/Cdr. C.L. Murphy, USN), USS Entemedor (T/Cdr. W.R. Smith, Jr., USN), USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN), USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN), USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN), USS Lamprey (T/Lt.Cdr. L.B. McDonald, USN), USS Lizardfish (T/Cdr. O.M. Butler, USN) and USS Loggerhead (T/Cdr. R.N. Metcalf, USN) departed Subic Bay. All were to proceed to Pearl Harbour except USS Blenny, USS Blueback and USS Boarfish which were to proceed to Guam.

14 Sep 1945 The submarine tender USS USS Howard W. Gilmore (T/Cdr. C.A. Johnson, USN) and the submarines USS Puffer (T/Lt.Cdr. C.R. Dwyer, USN), USS Rasher (T/Lt.Cdr. C.D. Nace, USN), USS Raton (T/Lt.Cdr. G.F. Gugliotta, USN), USS Baya (T/Lt.Cdr. B.C. Jarvis, USN), USS Becuna (T/Lt.Cdr. W.J. Bush, USN), USS Caiman (T/Cdr. W.L. Fey, Jr., USN), USS Capitaine (T/Lt.Cdr. E.S. Friedrick, USN), USS Carbonero (T/Cdr. C.L. Murphy, USN), USS Entemedor (T/Cdr. W.R. Smith, Jr., USN), USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN), USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN), USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN), USS Lamprey (T/Lt.Cdr. L.B. McDonald, USN), USS Lizardfish (T/Cdr. O.M. Butler, USN) and USS Loggerhead (T/Cdr. R.N. Metcalf, USN) arrived at Pearl Harbor from Subic Bay.

15 Sep 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN), USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN), USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN), USS Lamprey (T/Lt.Cdr. L.B. McDonald, USN), USS Lizardfish (T/Cdr. O.M. Butler, USN) and USS Loggerhead (T/Cdr. R.N. Metcalf, USN) departed Pearl Harbor for San Francisco.

22 Sep 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN), USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN), USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN), USS Lamprey (T/Lt.Cdr. L.B. McDonald, USN), USS Lizardfish (T/Cdr. O.M. Butler, USN) and USS Loggerhead (T/Cdr. R.N. Metcalf, USN) arrived at San Francisco from Pearl Harbor.
